svg.text: EGYPTIAN ARABIC
GRAMMAR — DIALECT VS STANDARD
EGYPTIAN ARABIC VERB SYSTEM
Prefix conjugation: bi- marks present/habitual (dialectal innovation)
Biyiktib "he writes (habitually)" • hayiktib "he will write"
Katab "he wrote" (past, same as MSA) • ha- = future prefix
MSA has no bi- present marker; this is Egyption/Levantine innovation
NEGATION: MISH AND MA...SH
Egyptian: ma + verb + sh = "not" (circumfix negation)
Makatab-sh "he didn't write" • mish ktib "not written"
-sh suffix: possible Coptic origin (Coptic negation: an/en)
MSA: la/lam + verb (no circumfix) — Egyptian negation unique
EGYPTIAN ARABIC WORD ORDER: SVO
Colloquial Egyptian: SVO (subject-verb-object)
MSA: VSO • Egyptian Arabic: SVO (parallel to Modern Hebrew shift)
Ana katab-t el-gawab "I wrote the letter" (SVO)
GREEK GRAMMAR · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
MODERN GREEK CASE SYSTEM
4 cases: nominative, accusative, genitive, vocative
Ancient Greek had 5 cases (added dative); Modern lost dative
Definite article agrees with noun in case, gender, number
GREEK VERB SYSTEM
Two aspects: imperfective and perfective (parallel to Semitic!)
Aspect rather than tense is the core distinction (like Arabic/Hebrew)
Three persons, two numbers; verb encodes subject (pro-drop language)
GRAMMAR COMPARISON
Egyptian Arabic: SVO, aspect-based verbs, circumfix negation
Greek: SVO (flexible), aspect-based verbs, 4-case nouns
Both: pro-drop (subject pronouns optional)
Both: aspect at core of verbal system (shared Mediterranean typology)
PLATE 60c ALT · EGYPTIAN ARABIC / GREEK · GRAMMAR
